title: Phase-space measurements and decoherence for angular momentum systems
authors: Dorje C. Brody, Eva-Maria Graefe, Rishindra Melanathuru
abstract: The monitoring of the three independent components of the angular momentum (or spin) of a quantum system by its environment that does not isolate any preferred orientation is modelled in two different ways. One describes the dynamics by the Lindblad equation generated by three independent angular momentum operators. The other uses iterated measurements of the ``phase-space'' point on the sphere in terms of the positive operator-valued measure generated by SU(2) coherent states. In contrast to the equivalent scenario on a flat phase space, these two models give rise to subtle differences. Specifically, it is shown that the two super-operators corresponding to the two decoherence models for angular momentum systems are commutative, but their eigenvalues are different. Hence although both models give rise to phase-space decoherence, their dynamical behaviours are not equivalent. In either model, we find that the characterisation of classicality as represented by the decay rates of the elements of the density matrix (i.e. decoherence) and that as represented by the positivity of the quasiprobability distribution are not equivalent for angular momentum systems.
summary: The paper demonstrates that continuous-time environmental monitoring and discrete phase-space measurements lead to distinct dynamical behaviors in angular momentum systems. It reveals that the two models yield different decay rates for spin multipoles and present conflicting definitions of the transition to classicality. This distinction is absent in spin-1/2 systems but becomes significant for higher spin magnitudes.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether the equivalence between continuous-time Lindblad dynamics and discrete POVM measurements found in flat phase spaces extends to the spherical phase space of angular momentum systems.
main_result: The authors show that the two models are not equivalent for angular momentum systems (except for spin-1/2), leading to different decay rates for multipole moments and a discrepancy in how classicality is defined via density matrix decay versus quasiprobability positivity.
method: The study uses an expansion in irreducible tensor operators, the Stratonovich-Weyl kernel for phase-space representations, and a microscopic derivation of the Lindblad equation via stochastic averaging over Gaussian white noise.
model_or_system: The system consists of quantum angular momentum (spin) systems of magnitude J, modeled via two approaches: a Lindblad master equation representing isotropic environmental monitoring and iterated nonselective POVM measurements using SU(2) coherent states.
key_observables: Decay rates of density matrix elements (multipole moments), multipole sectors (orientation and alignment), and the positivity of quasiprobability distributions (Wigner, Husimi Q, and Glauber-Sudarshan P functions).
important_parameters: Spin magnitude J, Lindblad decoherence rate gamma, number of measurement iterations n, and the parameter sigma for the family of quasiprobability distributions.
main_assumptions: The environment is modeled as isotropic Gaussian white noise with no preferred orientation, and the POVM measurements are assumed to be nonselective.
figures_summary: Figure 2 compares Wigner functions for different spin magnitudes (J=1 and J=5) under both models to show robustness; Figure 3 illustrates the decoherence of a highly quantum superposition state.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a microscopic derivation of the Lindblad equation from noise, compares the two decoherence models using tensor operator expansions, analyzes the decay of multipole moments, and concludes by discussing the implications for the emergence of classicality.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it identifies a fundamental breakdown of the 'flat phase space' intuition when applied to spin systems, specifically regarding how decoherence scales with spin magnitude.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Decoherence effect for J = 1 (left) and J = 5 (right). The components ρ20 and ρ10 of the density matrix, prepared initially in a coherent state, as functions of time/iteration number for the Lindblad dynamics (solid blue lines, with γ = 1/J) and repeated POVM measurements (black crosses) are shown here. The impact of decoherence in terms of the decay of the elements of the density matrix is less pronounced with coherent states having larger spins.

FIG. 2: Comparison of the decoherence models for an initial spin coherent state for J = 1 (top) and J = 5 (bottom). The left column shows the Wigner functions of initial coherent states with J = 1 and J = 5. The middle column shows the transformed Wigner functions after four iterations of POVM measurements. The right column shows the Wigner functions as the solution to the Lindblad evolution at time t = 4 (with γ = 1/J). The results illustrate the fact that features of a coherent state with higher spins are more robust against decoherence.
